coredata.dc:title: Niche nanoparticle-based FRET assay for bleomycin detection via DNA scission
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Haimeng
Zheng, Yiqun
Kong, Rongmei
Xia, Lian
Qu, Fengli
coredata.dc:description: We describe a highly sensitive nanoparticle–based fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe developed without using molecular fluorophores as donors and acceptors. The success of this work relies on the strategy that DNA scission was designed to occur to the probe when target presented, which enabled the fluorescence signal “turn-on” of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and thus quantitative analysis. In particular, amino-modified SiO2 NPs were initially coated by GQDs to form highly emitting SiO2/GQDs, followed by conjunction with DNA functionalized gold nanoparticles (Au NPs–DNA) to form SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs composite. Owing to the FRET interactions between the GQDs and Au NPs, the fluorescence of GQDs was effectively quenched by Au NPs. When bleomycin (BLM), a model analyte, was mixed with the probe, the fluorescence signal of GQDs would be restored due to the removal of Au NPs from the SiO2/GQDs surface by DNA scission treatment with BLM in the presence of Fe (II). The current FRET probe shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of BLM in the range from 0.5nM to 1μM with a detection limit of 0.2nM. The probe also shows satisfactory results for the analysis of clinical serum samples. This method provides versatility to the application of GQDs in FRET biosensing and could be potentially extended to other similar systems by replacing the linker between the GQDs and Au NPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Silica nanospheres
Bleomycin
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au NPs functionalized DNA (Au NPs–DNA)
Practical analysis in serum samples analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21375076, 21205068, 21505084 ), the International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of China ( 2015DFA50060 ), the Project of Shandong Province Science and Technology Program ( 2013GGX10207 , 2015GSF121031 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2014BM029 ) and the Project of University-industry Cooperation of Jining City ( 2014018 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
TEM images of (A) Au NPs, (B) GQDs, (C) SiO2/GQDs, (D) SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs, (E) SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs after reaction with 800nM BLM·Fe(II), (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) 10mg/mL SiO2, (b) 10mg/mL SiO2/GQDs, (c) 10mg/mL SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs, (d) SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs after reaction with 800nM Fe (II). (e) SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs after reaction with 800nM BLM·Fe(II). Inset
: The GQDs on the SiO2 NPs surface.
UV/vis spectra of (a) DNA, (b) Au NPs, (c) Au NPs–DNA, (d) SiO2 NPs, (e) GQDs, (f) SiO2/GQDs, (g) SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra responses of the biosensor to different concentrations of BLM·Fe(II). (B) The relationship between the fluorescence enhancement and the BLM·Fe(II) concentration. Inset The responses of the biosensor to BLM·Fe(II) at low concentrations. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.

Selectivity of the biosensor for BLM detection. The concentration of BLM·Fe(II) and other interference proteins were 1μM and 10μM, respectively. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.
Schematic illustrations of the sensing mechanism.

head.ce:title: Niche nanoparticle-based FRET assay for bleomycin detection via DNA scission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu, Shandong 273165, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Colloidal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, Shandong 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Colloidal Materials, Shandong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We describe a highly sensitive nanoparticle–based fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) probe developed without using molecular fluorophores as donors and acceptors. The success of this work relies on the strategy that DNA scission was designed to occur to the probe when target presented, which enabled the fluorescence signal “turn-on” of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and thus quantitative analysis. In particular, amino-modified SiO2 NPs were initially coated by GQDs to form highly emitting SiO2/GQDs, followed by conjunction with DNA functionalized gold nanoparticles (Au NPs–DNA) to form SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs composite. Owing to the FRET interactions between the GQDs and Au NPs, the fluorescence of GQDs was effectively quenched by Au NPs. When bleomycin (BLM), a model analyte, was mixed with the probe, the fluorescence signal of GQDs would be restored due to the removal of Au NPs from the SiO2/GQDs surface by DNA scission treatment with BLM in the presence of Fe (II). The current FRET probe shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of BLM in the range from 0.5nM to 1μM with a detection limit of 0.2nM. The probe also shows satisfactory results for the analysis of clinical serum samples. This method provides versatility to the application of GQDs in FRET biosensing and could be potentially extended to other similar systems by replacing the linker between the GQDs and Au NPs.

ce:section.ce:para: The bleomycins (BLMs) are a family of antitumor antibiotics, members of which are used clinically for the treatment of squamous cell carcinomas and other malignancies (Bozeman et al., 2012). The antitumor activity of BLM is believed to derive from its ability through a sequence selective, metal-dependent oxidative cleavage of double stranded DNA in the presence of oxygen (Povirk et al., 1989; Yin et al., 2010). DNA cleavage has been especially well characterized, and the mechanism of degradation and derived products have been described in detail (Rabow et al., 1990). Owing to the advantages of low myelosuppression and low immunosuppression, BLM is less toxic to the human body and of particular importance in the clinical treatment of a variety of cancers among the various antitumor drugs (Tisman et al., 1973). However, the dose-limiting side effect of BLM is the possibility for the renal and lung toxicity, which can further cause bad pulmonary brosis (Hay et al., 1991; Liu et al., 2008). Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a sensitive method to quantitatively determine the BLM content in both pharmaceutical analysis and clinical samples, to make full use of therapeutic efficacy and to weaken its toxicity.

Up to now, many methods have been developed for quantitatively monitoring the level of BLM, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Aszalos et al., 1981), radioimmunoassay (RIA) (Broughton and Strong, 1976), enzyme immunoassay (EIA) (Fujiwara et al., 1981), electrochemical detection (Yin et al., 2010), and colorimetric assay (Li et al., 2011; Qin et al., 2014). It is worthy to note that these methods usually are plagued with some shortcomings, such as complicated instruments for HPLC, harmful to the health for RIA, instability and sensitive to denaturalization for EIA, and tedious experimental procedures for electrochemical assay. Compared to counterparts, the fluorescent methods have attracted increasing attention owing to their remarkable features of high sensitivity, facile operation, low cost, fast response, on-site and real-time detection (Liu et al., 2009; Nagraj et al., 2009). Although BLM is employed clinically at a typically low dosage (5μM), the appearance of pulmonary toxicity is still unpredictable (Peng et al., 2013). Thus, to achieve the best therapeutic effect and to minimize the toxicity of BLM, it is highly desirable to develop simple and reliable fluorescent methods for determination trace amounts of BLMs in clinical analysis.

In the past few years, researchers have found that the BLM·Fe(II) complex binds with oxygen to generate BLM·Fe(III)OOH which can selectively degradate DNA via C4′-H atom abstraction from deoxyribose in the minor groove of DNA. The generally observed selective scission was occurred predominantly at 5′-GC-3′ or 5′-GT-3′ sites of DNA strand (Akiyama et al., 2008; Kong et al., 2015). Therefore, the current study was focused on the developing specific DNA scission based biosensing assays for BLM detection. For example, Li et al. applied the specific reaction in BLM detection using the fluorescence of dye-labeled DNA quenched by graphene oxide (Li et al., 2012). Gao et al. use dexonuclease III aided DNA recycling amplification for detection BLM (Chang et al., 2013). Recently, Chang et al. developed DNA–templated silver nanoclusters (DNA–AgNCs) as label-free fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of BLM (Chang et al., 2013). Although these results contributed to the progress in the detection of BLM, some drawbacks still remained. For instance, metal nanoclusters are limited because of their ease of oxidation and agglomeration. Traditional fluorophores are limited by high cost and photobleaching effect. Enzyme-based probes are easily denatured with temperature changes.

To overcome these limitation and further improve the accuracy of BLM detection, GQDs, as recently emerging carbon–based materials, which can be identified to be a promising alternative signal output material in biosensing for the detection of BLM. Compared to metal nanoclusters, organic dyes and exonuclease, GQDs are more excellent photostable, high brightness, long fluorescence lifetime and easier to overcome harsh environments (Qian et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2013). Luminescent quantum dots (QDs) could be modified on the surface of microparticles by a diverse range of chemical methods to form nanocomposites (Cho et al., 2010; Grinyte et al., 2015). The nanocomposites exhibited attractive advantages, such as enhanced optical properties. Hence, a novel highly sensitive nanoparticle–based fluorescent signal probe is applied, where GQDs are coated by radial equidistance on the surface of silica nanospheres (SiO2/GQDs). Since GQDs can be coated on SiO2 NPs surface at high concentration, the SiO2/GQDs composite possessed the significant fluorescent signal changes. The resultant highly emitting SiO2/GQDs nanocomposite exhibits attractive fluorescent performance, large surface area, good photostability, convenient separation, durability, feasibility for surface functionalization and manipulation. Based on the effectively fluorescence quenching ability of Au NPs, the SiO2/GQDs was further applied in conjunction with Au NPs functionalized DNA (Au NPs–DNA) to fabricate SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs composite microspheres which can be used as FRET probe.

Herein, we report the preparation of SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs composite nanospheres based FRET probe for detection of BLM with high sensitivity and specificity. Attachment of Au NPs–DNA to SiO2/GQDs completely quenched the GQDs emission through FRET interactions. However, in the presence of BLM with Fe (II), the ssDNA between SiO2/GQDs and Au NPs underwent an irreversible strand scission due to the occurrence of the redox reaction between BLM and Fe (II). Therefore, the Au NPs was away from the SiO2/GQDs surface and the fluorescence of GQDs was restored. The developed FRET probe shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of BLM in the range from 0.5nM to 1μM with a detection limit of 0.2nM. The new SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs FRET platform opens many new possibilities for using linkers between the GQDs and Au NPs that selectively respond to specific cleaving or displacement agents in the future. In addition, the FRET probe described in this work has no quencher label cost, easy preparation and rapid response, making it a promising alternative for the analysis of clinical serum samples, which demonstrated its potential for practical diagnostic applications.

Bleomycin (BLM) was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotech Co. Ltd. (Dalian, China). Gold (III) chloride hydrate (HAuCl4) and tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. (St. Louis, USA). (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), aqueous ammonia solution (NH3·H2O, 28%) and sodium citrate (Na3CA) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). The GQDs were obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The metal salts (Hg(NO3)2, CoCl2, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(Ac)2, FeCl3, and FeCl2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). DNA buffers involved in this work were composed of 200mM NaCl and 10mM PBS (pH 7.4). A buffer containing 0.1M PB (pH 8.0) and 2M NaCl was used for preparation of Au NPs–DNA. The pH measurements were carried out on a Mettler-Toledo Delta 320pH meter. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The serum samples were kindly provided by Qufu Normal University hospital and stored at 4°C. All solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water (resistance>18 MΩ·cm) from a Millipore system. DNA oligonucleotide used in this work was synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the sequence is shown as follows: 5′-HS-(CH2)6-ATGCTATAATATTAAT-(CH2)6-NH2-3′ Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips CM200 FEG microscope. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a UV-2501PC spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X–ray power diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a MiniFlex600 diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). All fluorescence measurements were carried out on an F-4600 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The instrument settings were chosen as follows: λex=350nm (slit 5nm), λem=465nm (slit 5nm), PMT detector voltage=700V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au NPs functionalized DNA (Au NPs–DNA)
ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) with an average size of 15nm were prepared via the citrate reduction method (Su et al., 2010). Briefly, 1mL of HAuCl4 (1.0wt%) solution was initially added to 99mL of boiled distilled water, and then 2.5mL of Na3CA (1.0wt%) was quickly injected into the solution. The mixture was boiled for about 10min until the color became claret, followed by being cooled at room temperature and stored at 4°C for further use.

The Au NPs–DNA was freshly prepared according to a previous report (Kong et al., 2009). Au NPs–DNA was prepared by addition of thiolated DNA (10μM, 100μL) in sterilized water into 1mL Au NPs aqueous solution. After standing at 4°C for 16h, the Au NPs–DNA conjugates were “aged” by gradually addition of 0.1M PB (pH 8.0) and 2M NaCl buffer every 8h until the solution contained 0.1M NaCl and 10mM PB. Then the solution was centrifuged for 20min at 4°C (12,000rpm) and the supernatant was removed. The red oily precipitate was washed with 10mM PBS, recentrifuged, and then redispersed in 10mM PBS buffer, stored at 4°C.

Monodispersed SiO2 nanospheres (SiO2 NPs) used as carriers for GQDs were prepared by using a slightly modified Stober process (Rossi et al., 2005). In a typical synthesis of 80nm SiO2 NPs, 4.0mL of TEOS was rapidly added into a mixture of 47mL of ethanol and 3.3mL of NH3·H2O (28%). The mixture was vigorously stirred for 24h. By fixing the concentration of TEOS and varying the concentration of NH3·H2O, the resulting particle sizes can be easily adjusted. Finally, the monodispersed SiO2 NPs were separated by centrifugation and washed with ethanol for several times. Dry SiO2 NPs were obtained by drying the residue at 45°C in vacuum.

For the preparation of SiO2/GQDs composites, 100mg of SiO2 NPs were first dispersed in 8mL of ethanol and treated with 1mL of APTES. After stirring for 6h, the suspension was centrifuged and washed with ethanol repeatedly for four times and the amino-functionalized SiO2 NPs were obtained. 20mL of GQDs solution (2mg/mL) was firstly sonicated for 10min and then EDC (50mM) and NHS (10mM) were added. The mixture was shaken on a vortex mixer for 2min and bath sonicated for another 15min After that, 100mg amine-modified SiO2 NPs were dispersed in the above mixture. The mixed suspension was slowly stirred at room temperature for 24h. Unbound GQDs were removed by successive centrifugation and the suspension was washed with PBS for several times. Finally, the obtained SiO2/GQDs composites were dispersed in PBS with a final concentration of 10.0mg/mL for further use.

The as-prepared SiO2/GQDs (10.0mg/mL) suspension was mixed with EDC (50mM) and NHS (10mM) for 15min Subsequently, Au NPs–DNA were added and then incubated for 2h to form the SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs bioconjugates. Unbound Au NPs–DNA were removed by successive centrifugation and the bioconjugates was washed with PBS for several times. Then the SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs bioconjugates was stored at 4°C for further use.

The BLM samples were prepared by mixing BLM with Fe (II) ion in 1:1M ratio. 50μL of BLM·Fe(II) at various concentrations were added into 100μL of SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs for 30min at 25°C. Then, the fluorescent signal located at 465nm was offered.

A novel FRET probe was developed based on SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs for detection of BLM via DNA strand scission. The sensing mechanism of the proposed probe for BLM detection is shown in Scheme 1. The GQDs with an average diameter of 1–2nm were covalently attached to amino-functionalized SiO2 NPs due to the interaction of the amine and carboxyl groups, forming highly luminescent SiO2/GQDs. SiO2/GQDs and Au NPs acted as donor and acceptor of FRET pairs, respectively. The single strand DNA (5′-HS-(CH2)6-ATGCTATAATATTAAT-(CH2)6-NH2-3′) was first conjugated to the surface of Au NPs via the Au–S bond. Then, the 3′-amine modified ssDNA were immobilized on the SiO2/GQDs surface to form the satellite structure bioconjugates. Due to the close proximity of Au NPs to SiO2/GQDs, the fluorescence of SiO2/GQDs can be effectively quenched. Upon the addition of BLM·Fe(II), the ssDNA between SiO2/GQDs and Au NPs underwent an irreversible strand scission at the scission site indicated by the black arrow, releasing a 3-mer oligonucleotide fragment from the SiO2/GQDs surface, which was responsible for fluorescence restoration of the sensing system. The fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the addition of increasing concentrations of BLM·Fe(II).

The morphologies of Au NPs, GQDs, SiO2/GQDs and the satellite structure SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs were characterized using TEM. The Au NPs were synthesized using the citrate reduction method. TEM imaging was performed to characterize the morphology and size of Au NPs. As shown in Fig. 1A, the synthesized Au NPs had good spherical shape and uniform size with the average diameter of 15nm. Fig. 1B shows the GQDs were uniform and quasi-spherical with an average size of 1–2nm. Fig. 1(C) shows a TEM image after covalent attachment to amino-modified SiO2 NPs with GQDs. As shown in the inset of Fig. 1(C), we also clearly observed that there were a large number of GQDs on the SiO2 NPs surface. As shown in Fig. 1(D), the SiO2/GQDs were surrounded by many small Au NPs–DNA to form a satellite structure bioconjugates. A large number of Au NPs–DNA were attached on the SiO2/GQDs surface after mixing with Au NPs–DNA and SiO2/GQDs, indicating that Au NPs–DNA could be attached to SiO2/GQDs to form SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs composite microspheres which were used as FRET probes. In contrast, when BLM·Fe(II) was introduced in the system, the ssDNA between SiO2/GQDs and Au NPs underwent an irreversible strand scission at the scission site, resulting in the removal of Au NPs from the SiO2 NPs surface, which was responsible for fluorescence signal “turn-on” of GQDs (Fig. 1(E)). The SiO2 NPs has no obvious fluorescence intensity before coupling with GQDs (line a in Fig. 1(F)). The strong fluorescence of the SiO2/GQDs was found when GQDs were covalently attached to amino-modified SiO2 NPs forming highly emitting SiO2/GQDs (line b in Fig. 1(F)). However, the strong fluorescence of the SiO2/GQDs was found effectively quenched by Au NPs–DNA when the SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs was formed (line c in Fig. 1F). In the presence of Fe(II) only, the quenched fluorescence of the SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs system demonstrated almost no change (line d in Fig. 1(F)). Upon the addition of BLM·Fe(II) to remove Au NPs from the SiO2/GQDs surface, the quenched fluorescence of the SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs could be obviously recovered (line e in Fig. 1(F)).

XRD patterns of SiO2 NPs, GQDs and SiO2/GQDs composite were shown in Fig. S1. For SiO2 NPs, a high-intensity sharp peak at 2θ=24.5° related to the amorphous silica diffraction pattern, which is coincident with the previous report (Hu et al., 2013). GQDs exhibited a broader (002) peak centered at 2θ=22° (Pei et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2014). For SiO2/GQDs composite, similar diffraction peaks as SiO2 indicated the coated GQDs did not change the amorphous diffraction pattern of SiO2. XRD patterns of SiO2/GQDs composite did not show apparently peak toward GQDs, which may be resulted from the small size of GQDs (blank line in Fig. S1).

The process of fabricating the FRET probe was monitored using UV/vis spectrometer step by step. Fig. 2 showed the absorbance spectra of aqueous suspensions of DNA, Au NPs, Au NPs–DNA, SiO2, GQDs, SiO2/GQDs and SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs, respectively. As shown in the spectra, DNA exhibited a major absorbance peak located at 260nm (line a in Fig. 2). Au NPs exhibited a characteristic absorbance peak located at 530nm (line b in Fig. 2). There were two peaks for the Au NPs–DNA (line c in Fig. 2), in which the peak at 275nm could be attributed to the presence of DNA and the peak at 530nm could be ascribed to the presence of Au NPs. There was no obvious absorbance peak of SiO2 before covalently attached to GQDs (line d in Fig. 2). GQDs exhibited a major absorbance peak located at 360nm (line e in Fig. 2). An absorbance peak located at 360nm could be observed in SiO2/GQDs, indicating that GQDs were covalently attached to the SiO2 surface efficiently (line f in Fig. 2). After the formation of the satellite structure, the peaks attributed to DNA, Au NPs–DNA and SiO2/GQDs were found in line g Fig. 2. These results indicated that the FRET biosensing process was successful and efficient. To further validate the successful labeling of Au NPs–DNA with SiO2/GQDs, the as-prepared DNA, Au NPs–DNA, SiO2/GQDs and SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs are all analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. S2 shows that DNA (line a in Fig. S2) has a peak in the range of 1545cm−1corresponding to N–H stretching and the stretching band of S–H at 2550cm−1. Noticeable change in the infrared spectra could be observed if DNA has induced the formation of Au NPs–DNA (line b in Fig. S2). The band belonging to S–H disappears in the spectra of Au NPs–DNA induced by the binding of DNA to Au NPs, indicating that Au–S interaction has really occurred. In the FT-IR spectrum of SiO2/GQDs (line c in Fig. S2), stretching vibrations of C–OH and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–NH–C were observed at 3450cm−1 and 1126cm−1, respectively. The high-intensity peaks at 1628cm−1 and 1405cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylateanions (–COO–), respectively. Furthermore, as depicted in the spectrum of SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs (line d in Fig. S2), the band at 3450cm−1 became wider, which was attributed to the increase of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The typical bands associated with –CONH– could be seen at around 1649cm−1 and 1577cm−1, reflecting the conjugation of the Au NPs–DNA onto SiO2/GQDs. These results were coincident with the UV/vis spectra.

Before building the standard BLM assay, some important parameters were optimized to obtain the best sensing performance. Since the fluorescence signal was offered by the GQDs attached onto SiO2 NPs surface, the amount of GQDs and the size of SiO2 used in the assay have substantial effect on the fluorescence intensity. To achieve sensitive detection, the amount of GQDs and the size of SiO2 NPs were optimized in this work. Fig. S3 showed the corresponding fluorescence responses to the different amount of GQDs and the different size of SiO2 NPs. We noticed that the fluorescence intensity enhanced with an increased concentration of GQDs until it reached to 2.0mg/mL. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased when the concentration of GQDs exceeded 2.0mg/mL. It may be due to fluorescence self-quenching of GQDs in close proximity to one another on the surface of the SiO2 microspheres. In addition, our group reported SiO2 NPs with a diameter of 40nm in previous work to synthesis brushes. The centrifugation was time-consuming for the SiO2 NPs with a diameter of 40nm (Zhao et al., 2016). In the same time, big size of SiO2 NPs could affect the dispersion ability in water. The diameter of 80nm for SiO2 NPs was chosen as a compromise. Therefore, GQDs with a concentration of 2.0mg/mL and SiO2 NPs with a diameter of 80nm were used in the further experiments.

The concentration of Au NPs–DNA was another important factor for efficiently quenched the fluorescent signal response. The effect of the concentration of Au NPs–DNA was investigated in Fig. S3B. For optimizing the concentration of Au NPs–DNA, 30μL of SiO2/GQDs was mixed with different volumes of Au NPs–DNA, and then buffer was added to make total volume of 100μL. The above solutions were incubated for 2h at room temperature before fluorescence measurement. As shown in Fig. S3B, the fluorescence intensity of SiO2/GQDs decreased notably with the addition of increasing volume of Au NPs–DNA until it reached 70μL, and kept to a stable value after then, which may be owing to the saturated binding between the SiO2/GQDs and the Au NPs–DNA. In the presence of 70μL of Au NPs–DNA, we were able to obtain a quenching efficiency of up to 96%. Therefore, 70μL of Au NPs–DNA was used for the detection of BLM in this work.

Since a transitional metal ion is usually required during the strand scission process, an experiment was performed to investigate the effect of different metal ions on DNA scission in Fig. S3C. The BLM samples were prepared by mixing 1µM BLM with different metal ions in a 1:1M ratio. Fig. S3C showed the fluorescence changes upon treatment with a mixture of metal ion (Hg2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ce3+, Ce4+ or Fe2+) and BLM, respectively. Obviously, Fe (II) is the most active metal ion in combination with BLM for pronounced fluorescence restoration.

To get the best performance, we also investigated the effect of different ratios of BLM and Fe (II) on the DNA scission efficiency. Fig. S3D showed the fluorescence changes upon treatment with a mixture of 1μM BLM and Fe (II) at different concentration ratios, respectively. It could be found that the fluorescence intensity enhanced with an increased concentration ratios until it reached to 1:1, which was chosen as the optimal ratio for further investigations.

A “turn-on” FRET strategy was developed for detection of BLM based on SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs FRET probe. Under the optimal conditions, the sensing performance of this system was evaluated by adding various concentration of BLM·Fe(II). Triplicate tests were conducted for each measurement, and the Standard Deviation (SDs) was shown as error bars. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity increased dramatically with increasing the concentration of BLM·Fe(II) from 0 to 10μM, suggesting that the SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs is effective for fluorescence detection of BLM via BLM·Fe(II) induced DNA strand scission. The fluorescence intensity was enhanced as the concentration of BLM·Fe(II) increased, which reached a plateau when the concentration of BLM·Fe(II) increased to 10μM. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3B, there was a good liner relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the BLM·Fe(II) concentration in the range of 0.5–1000nM (R=0.979). The linear regression equations is Y=212.78+0.72C[BLM·Fe(II)] (nM), R2=0.973. The detection limit was found to be 0.2nM at S/N=3 on the basis of the *3Sb/slope,*Sb is standard deviation of the blank samples.

The analytical performance of the proposed probe was compared with the previously reported methods for the BLM·Fe(II) detection documented in literature, which can be supported in Table S1. It could be found that our FRET probe exhibited a new labels and superiority regarding liner range and detection limit. The excellent performance of the proposed FRET probe was attributed to the use of the novel SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs FRET design.

High specificity for the target analyte is necessary for a new designed FRET sensing probe with potential applications in practical samples. To verify the specificity of the proposed fluorescent probe, the change of fluorescence intensity as induced by several potential interfering compounds was also studied under the same conditions. The BLM·Fe(II) solution at 1μM and interference substances at 10μM are measured by the fluorescent probe, respectively. Samples containing BLM·Fe(II) with three other antitumor drugs, including daunorubicin, mitomycin and dactinomycin were employed to investigate anti-interference ability by monitoring the fluorescence intensity changes, as shown in Fig. 4. Obviously, a higher fluorescence signal was observed with BLM at 1μM than those of other drugs at 10μM, indicating that this method has a good specificity selectivity for discriminating BLM from other antitumor drugs. The excellent specificity of the FRET probe is mainly dependent upon in the presence of BLM·Fe(II), an irreversible scission of DNA underwent through the BLM-induced oxidation cleavage.

Furthermore, the stability of the FRET probe is also a key factor in the practical application. In this work, the stability of the FRET probe was tested by storing of SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs probe in buffer at 4°C for 15 days and then used to detect BLM. The experimental results showed that around 89% of the initial sensitivity was remained after 15 days. The stability of the highly emitting SiO2/GQDs and the reliability of the FRET method both ensured the stability of the proposed FRET probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical analysis in serum samples analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the practical application potential of this FRET probe, a recovery test of BLM·Fe(II) in human serum was evaluated. The practical application of the designed probe was evaluated by determination of the recovery of spiked BLM·Fe(II) in five diluted human serum samples (1%). As shown in Table 1, 10nM, 100nM, 500nM, 1μM and 10μM of BLM·Fe(II) were added into the serum samples, respectively. The recovery of BLM·Fe(II) is ranged from 91.7–99.44%, and the RSD is ranged from 2.59–4.10%, which were satisfactory for quantitative assays performed in biological samples.

In summary, with the target–induced cleavage of DNA, a novel sensitive fluorescence “turn-on” assay based on SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs composite nanospheres for detection of BLM was developed. The success of this work relied on the use of highly emitting SiO2/GQDs to achieve feasibility for surface functionalization and the strategy of DNA scission when target presented to enable the fluorescence signal “turn-on” of GQDs. The as-prepared nanospheres and the fabrication process of the FRET biosensing platform were characterized using TEM and UV/vis spectrometer. The proposed FRET probe achieved excellent performance for BLM detection with high sensitivity, good selectivity and stability. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of BLM was in the range from 0.5nM to 1μM with a detection limit of 0.2nM. Moreover, the practical application of the probe was validated through the detection of BLM in serum samples with satisfactory results. As a biochemical model, the new SiO2/GQDs–DNA–Au NPs FRET platform opens many new possibilities for using linkers between the GQDs and Au NPs that selectively respond to specific cleaving or displacement agents in the future. Therefore, this approach is not only sensitive and selective but also convenient and generalizable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375076, 21205068, 21505084), the International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of China (2015DFA50060), the Project of Shandong Province Science and Technology Program (2013GGX10207, 2015GSF121031), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2014BM029) and the Project of University-industry Cooperation of Jining City (2014018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2016.04.085.
